parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -lık (and its vowel-harmony variants -lik, -luk, -lük) is a workhorse in Turkish. One of its most common jobs is turning a noun into a place associated with that noun — so kitap (book) becomes kitaplık (bookshelf or library). It also forms abstract nouns and role words, which you'll see in several other words here.

words.exampleTranslation: I read all the books in the bookcase.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -men/-man is an older Turkish agent-forming suffix, now mostly seen in a handful of established words like öğretmen (teacher) and çoban (shepherd). It works like -er in English: the one who does the action. In modern Turkish, -ci/-cı is more productive for this role.

words.exampleSentence: Öğretmenimiz bugün yeni bir konu anlattı.
words.exampleTranslation: Our teacher explained a new topic today.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ar/-er forms agent nouns from verb roots in Turkish — so yaz- (to write) becomes yazar (writer/author). It's equivalent to English -er in this role.

Here -lık attaches to an already-formed agent noun (yazar) to create an abstract noun denoting the profession or state. So yazarlık is the condition or profession of being a yazar — authorship, the craft of writing.

words.exampleTranslation: Writing was his greatest passion.
I am cooking a delicious meal in the kitchen.
words.flag: only 1 part — mutfak is borrowed from Arabic matbakh (مطبخ, place of cooking) and its internal structure is not transparent in Turkish

parts.homeLangDetails: Here -lik (the front-vowel harmony form of -lık) creates an abstract noun from the adjective temiz, just as English -ness turns 'clean' into 'cleanliness.'

words.exampleTranslation: Cleanliness is the foundation of health.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -lik turns the adjective güzel (beautiful) into an abstract noun, exactly like English -ness turns 'beautiful' into 'beauty' or 'beautifulness.'

words.exampleTranslation: The beauty of this city enchants everyone.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -ne/-hane ending comes from Persian khane (خانه), meaning 'house' or 'place.' You see it in a handful of Turkish words: hastane (hospital, literally 'place of the sick'), eczane (pharmacy), and dershane (classroom). It's not a living productive suffix in modern Turkish but is recognizable across these loanwords.

words.exampleTranslation: My mother went to the hospital yesterday.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -çi (and its vowel-harmony variants -ci, -cu, -cü, -çı, -çu, -çü) is Turkish's most productive 'person associated with X' suffix. It covers professions, sellers, and enthusiasts alike — so iş (work) gives işçi (worker/laborer). Think of it as a general-purpose -er or -ist.

words.exampleTranslation: The workers at the factory are on strike.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here the -ci suffix attaches to the verb stem öğren- (to learn) to produce öğrenci (learner, student). It's the same suffix as in işçi, doing its standard job of creating a person-noun from a base.

words.exampleTranslation: Every student was successful in the exam.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -kan/-ken/-gan/-gen forms adjectives meaning 'inclined to' or 'characterized by' a certain action. So çalış- (to work/strive) plus -kan gives çalışkan: someone who is naturally hardworking, inclined to work. It's similar to how English uses -ing in a stable adjective sense ('a striving person'), but -kan implies a habitual disposition.

words.exampleTranslation: She is a very hardworking student.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -cı suffix here turns yardım (help/aid) into a noun or adjective meaning 'the one who helps' or 'helping.' It functions like English '-er' in helper, or the adjective 'helpful' depending on context.

words.exampleSentence: O her zaman yardımcı olmaya hazır.
words.exampleTranslation: She is always ready to be helpful.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here -lık creates an abstract noun from arkadaş (friend), giving arkadaşlık (friendship) — the state or quality of being friends. This mirrors how English -ship turns 'friend' into 'friendship.'

words.exampleTranslation: Friendship is the most precious treasure in life.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -gi/-gı/-gu/-gü is a nominalizing suffix that turns a verb root into an abstract noun. From sev- (to love) it produces sevgi — love as a feeling or state. It's less common than -lik but appears in a handful of important emotional and sensory nouns in Turkish.

words.exampleTranslation: His love for his mother is very deep.

parts.homeLangDetails: From say- (to count) plus the agent suffix -ar/-er, giving sayar — one who counts or processes. So bilgisayar is literally 'information counter' or 'knowledge processor,' a transparent coinage made when Turkish needed a word for computer.

words.exampleTranslation: I installed a new program on my computer.
My phone is ringing, can you wait a moment?

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -luk (and its vowel-harmony variants -lik, -lık, -lük) turns adjectives or nouns into abstract nouns, much like English -ness or -hood. So mutlu (happy) → mutluluk (happiness).

words.exampleTranslation: Happiness is the greatest wealth.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the verb üzmek (to sadden, to distress). The stem üzün- captures the state of being saddened or troubled.

The suffix -tü (and its vowel-harmony variants -ti, -tı, -tu) forms abstract nouns from verb stems, expressing the resulting state or condition. Here it turns the sense of being grieved into the noun 'sadness.'

words.exampleTranslation: Everyone noticed the sadness in her eyes.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -luk (vowel-harmony variants: -lik, -lık, -lük) converts adjectives into abstract nouns. Here soğuk (cold) becomes soğukluk (coldness). It can also refer to figurative coldness, like emotional distance.

words.exampleSentence: İkimiz arasındaki soğukluk birkaç gündür devam ediyor.
words.exampleTranslation: The coldness between us has been going on for a few days.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -lık (vowel-harmony variants: -lik, -luk, -lük) converts adjectives into abstract nouns. Here sıcak (warm, hot) becomes sıcaklık (warmth, heat). It works equally for physical temperature and figurative warmth.

words.exampleTranslation: The heat in summer sometimes becomes unbearable.

parts.homeLangDetails: When -luk (and its variants) is attached to a profession or role noun rather than an adjective, it means something like '-hood', '-ship', or 'the practice/office of being a —'. So doktorluk is not just 'doctorness' but 'being a doctor, the medical profession.'

words.translationLong: the medical profession, doctoring, being a doctor
words.exampleSentence: Doktorluk çok sorumluluk gerektiren bir meslektir.
words.exampleTranslation: Being a doctor is a profession that requires a lot of responsibility.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ci (vowel-harmony variants: -cı, -cu, -cü, -çi, -çı, -çu, -çü) is one of Turkish's most productive suffixes. It attaches to a noun to mean 'a person who deals with, works with, or sells that thing.' So gazeteci is literally 'a newspaper person' — a journalist or reporter.

words.exampleTranslation: The journalist followed the event on the ground.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -cı (vowel-harmony variants: -ci, -cu, -cü, -çi, -çı, -çu, -çü) means 'a person who works with or at something.' Attached to fırın (oven, bakery), it gives fırıncı: the person who works the oven — a baker.

words.exampleTranslation: The baker wakes up early every morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ci (vowel-harmony variants) marks a person associated with something. Here it attaches to taksi (taxi) to make taksici: a taxi driver, someone whose work is the taxi.

words.exampleTranslation: The taxi driver got us to the airport on time.

parts.homeLangDetails: This -i is the accusative case suffix (vowel-harmony variants: -i, -ı, -u, -ü), marking a definite direct object. So berberi means 'the barber' as the object of a verb, e.g. berberi gördüm (I saw the barber). Without it, berber is just the bare noun.

words.exampleTranslation: I called the barber, but he was busy.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -cı (vowel-harmony variants) forms occupational nouns. Boya means paint or dye, so boyacı is the person who paints or dyes things — a painter (as in house painter or shoe shiner) or dyer.

words.exampleTranslation: The painter painted the house in two days.

parts.homeLangDetails: Hane is a borrowing from Persian خانه (khāne), meaning 'house' or 'place.' In Turkish it appears as a suffix forming compound nouns for places: eczanehane → eczane (pharmacy), yatakhane (dormitory), pastane (pastry shop). Here it turns çamaşır (laundry) into çamaşırhane: a place for doing laundry — a laundromat or laundry room.

words.exampleTranslation: The laundry room is located in the basement.
The children go to school every day.

parts.homeLangDetails: The causative verb stem from öğrenmek (to learn). Öğretmek means 'to cause to learn' — i.e., to teach. The stem öğret- carries this meaning of teaching or instruction.

The suffix -men (also -man) is an agentive suffix that forms nouns for people who perform an action. So öğret- (to teach) + -men = öğretmen (teacher). It is less productive than -ci but appears in several key occupational words.

The suffix -lik (vowel-harmony variants: -lık, -luk, -lük) attached to an occupational noun means 'the profession, role, or state of being that person.' So öğretmenlik is 'teacherhood' — the profession or act of being a teacher.

words.translationLong: the teaching profession, being a teacher
words.exampleTranslation: Teaching is a very valuable profession.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ıl (vowel-harmony variants: -il, -ul, -ül) is a passive voice marker in Turkish. It turns an active verb into a passive one: yazmak (to write) → yazılmak (to be written). This passive stem is then used as the base for further derivation.

This final -ı (vowel-harmony variants: -i, -u, -ü) functions as a participial/adjectival suffix, converting the passive verb stem into an adjective meaning 'written' or, as a noun, 'a written exam.' So yazılı means 'written (thing)' or colloquially 'written test.'

words.exampleTranslation: There is a written math exam tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root kon- (related to settling, placing oneself) combined with the reciprocal suffix -uş, giving konuşmak its sense of mutual exchange — people talking with each other. The reciprocal nuance is baked into the stem, so konuşmak inherently implies a back-and-forth.
The suffix -mak (vowel-harmony variant: -mek) is the standard Turkish infinitive ending, equivalent to English 'to —'. It appears on all dictionary forms of Turkish verbs.

words.translationLong: to speak, to talk, to converse
words.exampleTranslation: Talking with you is always nice.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to read or to recite. It also covers studying in context, as in 'üniversite okumak' (to study at university).

words.translationLong: to read, to study, to recite
words.exampleTranslation: I want to read a book every night.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to write. It also covers typing and spelling in the right context.

words.translationLong: to write, to type, to spell
words.exampleTranslation: I want to write you a letter.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to go. Note that the t softens to d in most conjugated forms (gidiyorum, gidecek), but the dictionary form preserves the t.

words.translationLong: to go, to leave, to travel
words.exampleTranslation: I have to go to school tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to come or to arrive. It is also used in many compound verb constructions, such as 'hoşuna gelmek' (to please someone).

words.exampleTranslation: I want to come home early in the evening.

parts.homeLangDetails: A very high-frequency root covering both 'take' and 'get/receive'. It also appears in compound verbs like 'yardım almak' (to receive help) and 'nefes almak' (to breathe, literally 'take breath').

words.translationLong: to take, to get, to receive, to buy
words.exampleTranslation: We need to get milk from the market.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to give. It pairs naturally with almak (to take/get) and appears in many compound expressions, such as 'karar vermek' (to make a decision, literally 'give a decision').

words.translationLong: to give, to hand over, to yield
words.exampleTranslation: I want to give her a gift.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to love or to like. It covers both deep affection and everyday preferences, so context tells you whether someone loves a person or just likes pizza.

words.translationLong: to love, to like, to be fond of
words.exampleTranslation: I love listening to music.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to know (a fact or skill). It is also the source of the extremely important ability suffix -abil-/-ebil- (can/be able to), so this root is a cornerstone of Turkish grammar.

words.translationLong: to know, to be aware of
words.exampleTranslation: I want to know the answer to this question.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to want or to wish. It is one of the most common verbs in Turkish and is frequently followed by another verb in its infinitive form, just as in English.

words.translationLong: to want, to wish, to desire
words.exampleTranslation: I don't know what you want.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to do or to make. It is one of the most versatile roots in Turkish, appearing in countless compound expressions.
The passive voice suffix, here in its back-vowel form -ıl-. It transforms an active verb into one where the subject receives the action rather than performs it, like English '-ed' or 'to be done'.

words.translationShort: to be done, to be made
words.translationLong: to be done, to be made, to happen
words.exampleTranslation: This work has to be done today.

parts.homeLangDetails: The core verb root meaning to work or to study. It carries both senses — professional work and academic studying — depending on context. It also means 'to try' or 'to strive' in some expressions.

words.translationLong: to work, to study, to strive, to try
words.exampleTranslation: You need to study a lot for the exam.

parts.homeLangDetails: This form already contains a reflexive suffix (-en) fused into the stem, derived from dinlemek (to listen) via the idea of quieting oneself. The resulting stem dinlen- means specifically 'to rest' or 'to relax'.

words.translationLong: to rest, to relax, to take a break
words.exampleTranslation: I want to rest at home on the weekend.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning clean or pure. It forms the base from which the verb is built by adding the verbalizing suffix.
A highly productive suffix that turns nouns and adjectives into verbs, similar to English '-en' or '-ify'. So temiz (clean) + -le = to clean, to make clean.

words.translationLong: to clean, to tidy up, to purify
words.exampleTranslation: I have to clean the house every Saturday.

parts.homeLangDetails: A noun meaning head or top, but also used metaphorically as the beginning or start of something. This dual meaning (physical head and figurative beginning) is what drives the verb's meaning.
The back-vowel form of the verbalizing suffix -le/-la, turning the noun baş (head/beginning) into a verb: to begin, to start.

words.translationLong: to begin, to start, to commence
words.exampleSentence: Yeni bir projeye başlamak heyecan verici.
words.exampleTranslation: Starting a new project is exciting.

parts.homeLangDetails: A root meaning to end or to be finished, seen in the intransitive bitmek (to end, to run out). The causative suffix -ir- makes it transitive: to finish something, to bring something to an end.

A causative suffix that makes the verb transitive, shifting it from 'to end' (bitmek) to 'to finish (something)' or 'to cause to end'. Similar to how English uses 'to finish' as a transitive counterpart to 'to be done'.

words.translationLong: to finish, to complete, to end, to use up
words.exampleTranslation: It is important to finish the homework on time.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root verb stem meaning 'to learn.' Related to öğretmek (to teach) — the same root with a causative twist.

words.translationLong: to learn, to study, to find out
words.exampleTranslation: I want to learn Turkish.

parts.homeLangDetails: The verb stem meaning 'to understand' or 'to grasp meaning.' Related to anlam (meaning, sense) — so there's a nice loop: the root of 'understand' is the same as the word for 'meaning.'

words.translationLong: to understand, to comprehend, to grasp
words.exampleTranslation: I want to understand you.

parts.homeLangDetails: The verb stem for thinking or pondering. Also appears in düşünce (thought, idea) — the noun form of this same root.

words.translationLong: to think, to consider, to reflect
words.exampleTranslation: I need to think a little.

parts.homeLangDetails: The verb stem meaning 'to say' or 'to tell.' Also the root of söylem (discourse, speech) and söz (word, promise) — though söz involves a vowel shift at the root level.

words.translationLong: to say, to tell, to speak, to utter
words.exampleTranslation: What do you want to tell me?

parts.homeLangDetails: A privative suffix meaning 'without' or '-less.' Works like English '-less': susuz = waterless, parasız = penniless, işsiz = jobless.
A suffix forming abstract nouns from adjectives, much like English '-ness.' Susuz (waterless) + -luk = the state of being waterless, i.e. thirst.

words.exampleSentence: Susuzluk hissediyorum, bir bardak su içebilir miyim?
words.exampleTranslation: I'm feeling thirsty — can I have a glass of water?

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning 'hungry' or 'starving.' It also means 'open' in a different context (as in a door being open), but in this compound the 'hungry' sense is what drives the meaning.
A suffix forming abstract nouns from adjectives, much like English '-ness.' Here it turns the adjective aç (hungry) into the noun açlık (hunger, the state of being hungry).

words.exampleTranslation: We must fight hunger in the world.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning 'tired' or 'weary.' The root yormak means 'to tire (someone out),' and yorgun is the resulting state — the one who has been worn out.
A suffix forming abstract nouns from adjectives. Yorgun (tired) + -luk = yorgunluk, the state of being tired — fatigue or weariness.

words.exampleTranslation: I can't keep my eyes open from tiredness.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective (and noun) meaning 'sick,' 'ill,' or 'patient.' Borrowed from Persian. It stands on its own as a word — hasta = a sick person or patient.
A suffix forming abstract nouns. Hasta (sick) + -lık = hastalık, the state of being sick — illness or disease.

words.exampleTranslation: This disease is spreading very quickly.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning 'free' or 'independent.' Derived from öz (self, essence) — so özgür carries the sense of being one's own self, unrestrained by others.
A suffix forming abstract nouns — the vowel shifts to -lük here due to vowel harmony with özgür. Özgür (free) + -lük = özgürlük, freedom or liberty.

words.exampleTranslation: Freedom is everyone's right.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning 'alone,' 'lonely,' or 'only.' It can be neutral (simply 'alone, by oneself') or carry an emotional weight of loneliness depending on context.
A suffix forming abstract nouns. Yalnız (alone/lonely) + -lık = yalnızlık, the state of being alone — loneliness or solitude.

words.exampleTranslation: You can feel loneliness even in big cities.

parts.homeLangDetails: An older or archaic root meaning 'dark' or 'darkness,' not commonly used independently in modern Turkish. The full modern adjective is karanlık itself, but the root is visible in related forms across Turkic languages.
A suffix forming abstract nouns and adjectives. Here it builds karanlık from the root karan, giving both the adjective 'dark' and the noun 'darkness' — the word does double duty.

words.exampleTranslation: I can't see anything in the dark.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning 'bright,' 'light,' or 'enlightened.' It also functions as a noun meaning an intellectual or enlightened person — a nice double meaning rooted in the idea of light and clarity.
A suffix forming abstract nouns and adjectives. Aydın (bright) + -lık = aydınlık, both the noun 'brightness/light' and the adjective 'bright/well-lit.'

words.exampleTranslation: The brightness of this room is very beautiful.

parts.homeLangDetails: A privative suffix meaning 'without.' Ses (sound) + -siz = sessiz, 'soundless' or 'silent.' The double -s- is a result of the suffix attaching to a root ending in -s.
A suffix forming abstract nouns. Sessiz (silent) + -lik = sessizlik, the state of being silent — silence or quietness.

words.exampleSentence: Geceleri bu mahallede tam bir sessizlik olur.
words.exampleTranslation: At night there is complete silence in this neighborhood.

parts.homeLangDetails: A suffix meaning 'having' or 'full of' something — like English '-ful' or '-ed' in 'colorful' or 'colored.' Renk (color) + -li = renkli, colorful or colored.

words.exampleTranslation: I really love this colorful picture.

parts.homeLangDetails: A privative suffix meaning 'without.' Renk (color) + -siz = renksiz, 'colorless' or 'without color.' Works just like English '-less' here.

words.exampleSentence: Su renksiz ve tatsız bir sıvıdır.
words.exampleTranslation: Water is a colorless and tasteless liquid.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(l)ü (and its vowel-harmony variants -lı, -li, -lu) attaches to nouns to mean 'having' or 'full of' that quality — very similar to English -ful or -ed. So güçlü = 'having strength', umutlu = 'having hope'.

words.exampleTranslation: He/She is a very strong athlete.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(s)üz (and its vowel-harmony variants -sız, -siz, -suz) means 'without' or 'lacking' — a direct parallel to English -less. Güçsüz = 'without strength', umutsuz = 'without hope'.

words.exampleSentence: Hasta olduğumda kendimi çok güçsüz hissediyorum.
words.exampleTranslation: When I am sick, I feel very weak.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(l)u (vowel-harmony variant of -lü/-lı/-li) means 'having' or 'full of' the noun it attaches to — like English -ful. Umutlu = 'hopeful', güçlü = 'powerful'.

words.exampleTranslation: I feel hopeful about the future.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(s)uz (vowel-harmony variant of -süz/-sız/-siz) means 'without' or 'lacking' — parallel to English -less. Umutsuz = 'hopeless', güçsüz = 'powerless'.

words.exampleTranslation: He/She felt hopeless in this situation.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(l)uk (and its vowel-harmony variants -lık, -lik, -lük) forms abstract nouns, often corresponding to English -hood or -ness. It turns a noun into a state or period: çocukluk = 'childhood', gençlik = 'youth', dostluk = 'friendship'.

words.exampleTranslation: I miss my childhood memories.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(l)ik (vowel-harmony variant of -luk/-lık/-lük) forms abstract nouns from adjectives or nouns — like English -hood or -ness. Gençlik = 'youth/youngness', yaşlılık = 'old age'.

words.exampleTranslation: The energy of youth is a wonderful thing.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(l)ı (vowel-harmony variant of -lü/-lu/-li) means 'having' the quality of the noun. Yaşlı = 'having age' = 'old/aged'. Note that yaşlı is an intermediate derived form — the -lık suffix is then added to it to form the abstract noun.
Added here to the adjective yaşlı ('old/aged') to form the abstract noun 'old age' — the state or condition of being old. Same suffix pattern as in gençlik and çocukluk.

words.exampleTranslation: Health is very important in old age.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(l)uk (vowel-harmony variant of -lık/-lik/-lük) forms abstract nouns. When attached to words for people or relationships, it works like English -ship or -hood: dostluk = 'friendship', düşmanlık = 'enmity'.

words.exampleSentence: Onlarla uzun yıllar süren bir dostluk kurduk.
words.exampleTranslation: We built a friendship with them that lasted many years.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(l)ık (vowel-harmony variant of -luk/-lik/-lük) forms abstract nouns. Here it turns 'enemy' into the abstract state of being enemies — 'enmity' or 'hostility'. Compare dostluk (friendship) from dost (friend).

words.exampleTranslation: The hostility between them lasted for years.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -daş (and its vowel-harmony variants -taş, -deş, -teş) means 'sharer of' or 'fellow' — someone who shares the thing indicated by the root. Vatandaş = 'fellow of the homeland' = citizen/compatriot. The same suffix appears in meslektaş (colleague, fellow professional) and yoldaş (comrade, fellow traveler).

words.exampleSentence: Her vatandaşın oy kullanma hakkı vardır.
words.exampleTranslation: Every citizen has the right to vote.
The capital of Turkey is Ankara.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(c)ı (and its vowel-harmony variants -ci, -cu, -cü, -çı, -çi, -çu, -çü) forms agent nouns — a person associated with or working with the thing named. Kapıcı = 'door person' = doorman/janitor. Very common: araba (car) → arabacı (taxi/carriage driver), süt (milk) → sütçü (milkman).

words.exampleTranslation: The janitor cleans the building every morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the verb beklemek (to wait, to watch over, to guard). The root bek- carries the sense of watchful waiting. It is related to the same idea as a night watchman keeping vigil.

The suffix -(ç)i (vowel-harmony variant of the agent suffix -cı/-ci/-cu/-cü etc.) forms a person who does or is associated with the root action. Bekçi = 'one who watches/guards'. Compare kapıcı (doorman) from kapı (door).

words.exampleTranslation: The night watchman patrolled the park.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -(ç)ı (vowel-harmony variant of the agent suffix -cı/-ci/-cu etc.) means 'one who practices' the noun. Sanatçı = 'one who practices art' = artist. The same suffix in bekçi (watchman), kapıcı (doorman), and müzisyen's Turkish equivalent müzikçi.

words.exampleTranslation: He/She is a talented artist.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the stem form of müzik (music), adapted through French musicien. Turkish borrowed the whole word from French, so the internal structure here reflects French/Latin morphology rather than native Turkish word-building. Compare the native Turkish pattern in sanatçı (artist), where the agent suffix -çı is added directly to sanat (art).

This ending comes from the French suffix -ien (as in musicien), which corresponds to English -ian or -ist. It is not the native Turkish agent suffix -cı/-çı, but a borrowed suffix that came packaged with the word. A few other French-origin borrowings in Turkish end similarly.

words.exampleTranslation: My sibling is a professional musician.
Her naturalness impresses everyone.
The artificiality in his speech was immediately obvious.
This application is very useful.
This tool is completely useless.
Smoking is harmful to health.
This insect is completely harmless.

words.exampleSentence: Bugün çok verimli bir gün geçirdim.
words.exampleTranslation: I had a very productive day today.
It is difficult to farm on unproductive land.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here -lik forms an abstract noun from güven (trust, safety), giving the sense of a state or condition — safety, security. It's the same suffix as the -lık in doğallık or büyüklük, just with vowel harmony adjusting the vowel.

words.exampleTranslation: Security cameras were installed everywhere.

parts.homeLangDetails: The -e here is not a productive affix but part of the frozen word tehlike (danger). The word is borrowed from Arabic (tahlika), and -e is not independently meaningful in Turkish — it is simply the final syllable of the root as it was absorbed into Turkish.

words.exampleSentence: Bu yol çok tehlikeli, dikkatli ol.
words.exampleTranslation: This road is very dangerous, be careful.
words.flag: only 1 part — tehlike is an Arabic loanword with no productive Turkish morpheme boundary

words.exampleSentence: Bu programın kolaylığı sayesinde işimi çok çabuk bitirdim.
words.exampleTranslation: Thanks to the ease of this program, I finished my work very quickly.
You can overcome every difficulty in life.
The size of this painting surprised me.

words.translationLong: smallness, littleness; also used to mean childhood (the state of being little)
words.exampleTranslation: I used to read a lot of books in my childhood.
The length of this bridge is two kilometers.
The brevity of the answer surprised everyone.
The weight of this bag is too much.
The lightness of the feather surprised me.
The hardness of the stone made it difficult to break.
The softness of the velvet was truly impressive.
The fast car passed through the city center.
The car moved slowly.
Careful drivers prevent accidents.
Careless behavior can lead to dangerous consequences.

parts.homeLangDetails: A core Turkish word meaning 'surroundings,' 'environment,' or 'neighborhood.' It comes from the verb 'çevirmek' (to turn, to surround) — so 'çevre' is literally 'what surrounds you.'
The -ci suffix here creates 'çevreci' (environmentalist) — someone who advocates for or is devoted to the environment. It is the same suffix that makes 'çay' (tea) into 'çaycı' (tea seller).
The -lik suffix turns 'çevreci' (environmentalist) into an abstract noun: environmentalism as a movement, ideology, or set of practices.

words.exampleSentence: Çevrecilik son yıllarda giderek daha fazla önem kazanmaktadır.
words.exampleTranslation: Environmentalism has been gaining more and more importance in recent years.
